citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Female Shih tzu (tag #084) has heavy matting on the lower part of all four legs surrounding the feet. The mats are firm,
heavy, and comingled with fecal material. Several of the mats were tight against the skin and the matted hair is so thick
that it was hard for the inspector to find the toenails. When the dog moved around the enclosure the mats drug along on
the flooring.
Female poodle (tag #083) has heavy matting along the back, around the face, and down the hind legs. The mats are solid
and several are tight against the skin.
Matted hair coats can cause pain and discomfort to the animal as well as lead to further health issues.
Each dealer must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee
must have the matted hair removed from the dogs' hair coats. The licensee must ensure the hair coats of each dog
remain free of matted hair at all times.
Correction date: The dogs were groomed at the time of the inspection.
The licensee has a plan to maintain healthy and unmatted hair coats, but 2 dogs were identified with heavily matted hair
coats. The licensee stated that the dogs are groomed at least every 6 months or more often, and the plan to maintain
healthy and unmatted hair coats (documented in the Program of Veterinary Care) says that the dogs will be bathed and
brushed when necessary. Failure to have plan that maintains clean and unmatted hair coats could result in hair coats that
are dirty, matted, and causing pain and discomfort to the animals.
Each dealer must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee
must establish an effective plan that maintains hair coats in a healthy and unmatted condition. The licensee must ensure
that all preventative care and treatment plans are effective at all times.
Correction date: The licensee updated the plan at the time of the inspection.

Two adult dogs, shih tzu #084 and poodle #083, do not have identification. The facility uses tags attached to a collar
around the dog's neck, but neither dog has a collar or tag. Failure to have identification on each dog can interfere with the
ability of the APHIS official to identify the animal, track the movements of the animal, and determine which medical
records belong to the animal.
All live dogs shall be identified by an official tag of the type described in § 2.51 affixed to the animal's neck by means of a
collar made of material generally considered acceptable to pet owners as a means of identifying their pet dogs or shall be
identified by a distinctive and legible tattoo marking acceptable to and approved by the Administrator. The licensee must
identify the 2 dogs with one of the above mentioned means of identification. The licensee must ensure each dog has
compliant identification at all times.
Correction date: A collar and tag was placed on each dog at the time of the inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Emily Steele, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Female Shih tzu (tag #084) has heavy matting on the lower part of all four legs surrounding the feet. The mats are firm,

that it was hard for the inspector to find the toenails. When the dog moved around the enclosure the mats drug along on 
the flooring.  
 
Female poodle (tag #083) has heavy matting along the back, around the face, and down the hind legs. The mats are solid 
and several are tight against the skin.  
 
Matted hair coats can cause pain and discomfort to the animal as well as lead to further health issues.  
 
Each dealer must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee 
must have the matted hair removed from the dogs' hair coats. The licensee must ensure the hair coats of each dog 
remain free of matted hair at all times.

The licensee has a plan to maintain healthy and unmatted hair coats, but 2 dogs were identified with heavily matted hair

Each dealer must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee 
must establish an effective plan that maintains hair coats in a healthy and unmatted condition. The licensee must ensure 
that all preventative care and treatment plans are effective at all times. 
 
Correction date: The licensee updated the plan at the time of the inspection. 
2.50(a)(1)

Time and method of identification. 
Two adult dogs, shih tzu #084 and poodle #083, do not have identification. The facility uses tags attached to a collar 
around the dog's neck, but neither dog has a collar or tag. Failure to have identification on each dog can interfere with the 
ability of the APHIS official to identify the animal, track the movements of the animal, and determine which medical 
records belong to the animal.  
 
All live dogs shall be identified by an official tag of the type described in § 2.51 affixed to the animal's neck by means of a 
collar made of material generally considered acceptable to pet owners as a means of identifying their pet dogs or shall be 
identified by a distinctive and legible tattoo marking acceptable to and approved by the Administrator. The licensee must 
identify the 2 dogs with one of the above mentioned means of identification. The licensee must ensure each dog has 
compliant identification at all times.  
 
Correction date: A collar and tag was placed on each dog at the time of the inspection. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Emily Steele, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
